questions.question: What does Shannon entropy H(X) represent?
options: The maximum possible compression ratio
The minimum average bits per symbol to encode a source
The expected prediction accuracy of a model
The total information content of a dataset
questions.explanation: Shannon entropy H(X) = -Σ p(x) log₂ p(x) gives the theoretical minimum average bits per symbol needed to encode the source. This is the source coding theorem.

questions.question: If H(p, q) is cross-entropy and H(p) is entropy, what is D_KL(p || q)?
options: H(p) - H(p, q)
H(p, q) - H(p)
H(p) + H(p, q)
H(p, q) / H(p)
questions.explanation: D_KL(p || q) = H(p, q) - H(p). It represents the extra bits needed when using model q instead of the true distribution p.

questions.question: Why is BPB (bits-per-byte) used instead of cross-entropy loss for the Parameter Golf leaderboard?
options: BPB is easier to compute
BPB is always lower than cross-entropy
BPB is tokenizer-agnostic, enabling fair comparison across different vocabularies
BPB accounts for model size in its calculation
questions.explanation: Different tokenizers produce different numbers of tokens per document. BPB normalizes by bytes instead of tokens, so a 1024-vocab model and a 32K-vocab model are compared on the same scale.

questions.question: What is perplexity in terms of cross-entropy H(p,q) (in nats)?
options: e^H(p,q)
log(H(p,q))
questions.explanation: When cross-entropy is measured in nats (natural log), perplexity = e^H(p,q). When in bits (log base 2), perplexity = 2^H.

questions.question: The statement 'compression = prediction' means:
options: Compressed models are better predictors
A perfect next-token predictor achieves the optimal compression rate
You must compress the model before evaluating prediction quality
Prediction accuracy improves with smaller file sizes
questions.explanation: The source coding theorem shows that a model achieving H(p,q) = H(p) is both a perfect predictor and an optimal compressor. Minimizing cross-entropy simultaneously optimizes both.

questions.question: In scaled dot-product attention, why do we divide by sqrt(d_k)?
options: To normalize the output to unit variance
To prevent dot products from growing large, keeping softmax in a well-behaved regime
To make the computation faster on GPUs
To ensure the attention weights sum to 1
questions.explanation: When d_k is large, the dot products QK^T can have large magnitude, pushing softmax into regions with vanishingly small gradients. Scaling by 1/sqrt(d_k) keeps the variance of the dot products around 1.

questions.question: What is the key property of RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding)?
options: It uses learned position embeddings that are added to the input
It makes the attention score q_m^T k_n depend only on relative position (m-n)
It applies different frequencies to different layers
It eliminates the need for position information entirely
questions.explanation: RoPE rotates query and key vectors by position-dependent angles. The inner product q_m^T k_n depends on the angle difference, which is proportional to (m-n), making it a relative position encoding.

questions.question: In the Parameter Golf baseline, what is the MLP activation function?
options: GELU
SwiGLU
ReLU^2 (squared ReLU)
Sigmoid
questions.explanation: The baseline uses ReLU^2: FFN(x) = W_2 * (ReLU(W_1 x))^2. The SOTA uses LeakyReLU(0.5)^2.

questions.question: What does the causal mask enforce in autoregressive attention?
options: Each token can only attend to itself
Each token can attend to all other tokens
Token at position i can only attend to positions 1 through i
Attention weights are uniformly distributed
questions.explanation: The causal mask sets attention weights to 0 (via -infinity before softmax) for all future positions j > i. This ensures the model can only use past context for prediction.

questions.question: Why are residual connections important in deep transformers?
options: They reduce the number of parameters
They speed up inference
They create direct gradient paths preventing vanishing gradients
They improve the tokenizer's vocabulary coverage
questions.explanation: With y = x + F(x), the gradient dy/dx = 1 + dF/dx always has a +1 component. This prevents gradients from vanishing through many layers, enabling training of deep networks.

questions.question: In Kaplan et al. scaling laws, L(N) represents:
options: Loss as a function of training time
Loss as a function of parameter count, following a power law
Loss as a function of learning rate
Loss as a function of batch size
questions.explanation: L(N) = (N_c / N)^alpha_N describes how loss decreases as a power law with increasing parameter count N, when data and compute are unconstrained.

questions.question: What did the Chinchilla paper (Hoffmann et al.) demonstrate?
options: Bigger models are always better
For a fixed compute budget, parameters and data should be scaled roughly equally
Small models cannot achieve good perplexity
Quantization is necessary for efficient training
questions.explanation: Chinchilla showed that previous practice (GPT-3) over-allocated to parameters. For compute-optimal training, N and D should scale proportionally: N ~ C^0.5, D ~ C^0.5.

questions.question: In Parameter Golf, why is the L(N) framing useful but incomplete?
options: Because the dataset is too small
Because the architecture is fixed
Because N is measured in compressed bytes, not raw parameters, and architecture/quantization are free
Because there is no compute constraint
questions.explanation: Standard scaling laws assume standard transformers. In Parameter Golf, N is measured as compressed artifact size, and techniques like quantization, weight sharing, and better compression shift the effective L(N) curve.

questions.question: At fixed parameter count, which generally produces lower loss?
options: Wider model (fewer layers, larger hidden dim)
Deeper model (more layers, smaller hidden dim)
They are always equivalent
It depends entirely on the dataset
questions.explanation: Research and the Parameter Golf leaderboard show that deeper models are generally more parameter-efficient. The leaderboard evolved from 9 layers (baseline) to 11 layers, trading width for depth.

questions.question: The 10-minute training constraint on 8xH100 means:
options: You can only train for 10 steps
Faster architectures can fit more training steps, creating a training efficiency tradeoff
All submissions train for the same number of steps
The constraint only applies to evaluation
questions.explanation: At ~87ms/step (SOTA), you get ~6900 steps in 600 seconds. A technique that improves loss-per-step but doubles step time may be net negative because you only get half as many steps.

questions.question: In GQA with 8 query heads and 4 KV heads, how many query heads share each KV head?
questions.explanation: With 8 query heads and 4 KV groups, each KV head is shared by 8/4 = 2 query heads. This halves the KV parameter cost compared to full multi-head attention.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of Flash Attention over standard attention?
options: It approximates attention for faster computation
It computes exact attention with O(T) memory instead of O(T^2) by tiling in SRAM
It reduces the number of attention heads needed
It eliminates the need for the causal mask
questions.explanation: Flash Attention tiles the Q, K, V computation into blocks that fit in SRAM, computing partial softmax results without materializing the full T x T attention matrix in HBM. It is exact, not approximate.

questions.question: For the 512d / 8-head baseline with GQA-4, how many parameters are in the K and V projections per layer?
questions.explanation: With 4 KV heads at head_dim=64, kv_dim = 4 x 64 = 256. So K and V each need 256 x 512 = 131,072 params. Total K+V = 262,144 params per layer.

questions.question: Flash Attention 3 specifically targets which GPU architecture?
options: NVIDIA Ampere (A100)
NVIDIA Hopper (H100) with warp specialization and TMA
NVIDIA Blackwell (B200)
AMD MI300X
questions.explanation: Flash Attention 3 uses Hopper-specific features: warp specialization (producer-consumer warps), Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA), and FP8 accumulation for maximum throughput on H100 GPUs.

questions.question: With tied embeddings at d=512, how many parameters does a 1024-token vocabulary cost?
questions.explanation: With tied embeddings, the embedding table is shared with the output projection: V x d = 1024 x 512 = 524,288 params. Without tying, it would be 2x.

questions.question: Why does the SOTA use a 1024-token vocabulary + BigramHash instead of a larger vocabulary?
options: Larger vocabularies are not supported by SentencePiece
Small vocab saves embedding parameters, BigramHash adds n-gram context cheaply via hashing
Larger vocabularies always produce worse BPB
The rules prohibit vocabularies larger than 1024
questions.explanation: A 1024-token vocab costs only 524K params (3% of budget). BigramHash adds 344K params for 3072 bigram entries at 112d. Together they approximate a much larger vocab at a fraction of the parameter cost.

questions.question: What makes BPB tokenizer-agnostic?
options: It only counts ASCII bytes
It normalizes by bytes instead of tokens, so different vocab sizes produce comparable scores
It uses a fixed reference tokenizer for all submissions
It ignores the tokenizer entirely
questions.explanation: BPB = (bits_per_token) x (tokens/bytes). A large vocab has fewer tokens but more bits per token. Normalizing by bytes cancels out the tokenizer's effect on token count.
